conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances. Let me start by gathering some information.

Do you have any children that you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives with me. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with you.

To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or an approved training course)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He finished school and decided not to continue with A-levels or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Before I can make a final determination, I need to read the official eligibility rules to ensure I give you accurate information.

Let me check the government guidance on Child Benefit eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Based on the official government guidance I've just reviewed and the information you've provided, I now need to ask you one more question:

Is Alex currently working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. He's working full-time at a local shop - it's more than 24 hours a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I now have enough details to make a determination about your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official UK government rules, I need to inform you of the outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: While you normally qualify for Child Benefit for children under 16, Alex is 17. For children aged 16 or over (but under 20), they must be in approved education or training for you to continue receiving Child Benefit.

Since Alex is:
- Not in approved education or training, AND
- Working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment

You do not qualify for Child Benefit for him. The government rules specifically state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he decides to go back into education or training and reduces his working hours), you may become eligible at that point.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and not in approved education or training. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16, OR if the child is 16 or over (but under 20) and stays in approved education or training. Since Alex is not in approved education or training, AND he is working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment, you are not eligible for Child Benefit. The government guidance specifically states: 'You'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.' This applies to Alex's situation.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with them. Alex is not in approved education or training and is working full-time (more than 24 hours per week). Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility for children aged 16-19 requires them to be in approved education or training. Additionally, Child Benefit stops when a child aged 16 or over works 24 hours or more per week and is not in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

The government guidance specifically states: 'You'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.' This applies to Alex's situation.

Alex is not in approved education or training and is working full-time (more than 24 hours per week). Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility for children aged 16-19 requires them to be in approved education or training. Additionally, Child Benefit stops when a child aged 16 or over works 24 hours or more per week and is not in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
